title: Configuration
scan: What you will learn: The decision framework for placing any value in code or config — and why the boundary is an architectural choice, not a convenience
Why it matters: Over-configured systems are as unmaintainable as over-hardcoded ones — the decision boundary determines how much an author can change without an engineer
Config is the correct location for thresholds, labels, feature flags, API endpoints, timing values — code is correct for algorithms, data transformations, security logic
A config value with no validation schema is a runtime bug waiting to happen — validate all config on load

blocks.html: odern enterprise software is code plus configuration. A surprising amount of what looks like business logic should actually live in config. Knowing the line is what separates engineers from architects.

Before

Animation duration hardcoded as the integer 300 in five separate components. Changing the design system's timing requires a code change, a PR, a deploy.

After

Single --animation-duration CSS variable in a shared config file. One line changes, all five components update. No code change, no PR, no deploy.

Putting business logic in config. A config value that controls branching behaviour — if config.mode === "enterprise", do X — is code disguised as data. It cannot be tested in isolation and creates hidden dependencies between config values. Extract to a Strategy function; the config value selects the strategy, not the behaviour.

cards.title: Feature flags
cards.body: Turn capabilities on or off without a deploy.

cards.title: Thresholds & limits
cards.body: Batch sizes, retry counts, timeouts, rate caps.

cards.title: Permissions & rules
cards.body: Business rules that change with regulation, market or persona.

columns.label: As code
columns.body: Type-safe, reviewable, version-controlled. Requires a deploy to change. Good for invariants that should never change without engineering sign-off.

columns.label: As config
columns.body: Changeable at runtime, faster for ops, supports A/B and gradual rollouts. Needs auditing, validation and fallbacks because it can break silently.

blocks.html: The architect's question on every value: does this change with the business, the environment or the user — or is it a property of the code itself? The first three go into config; only the last stays as code.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Configuration
items: Where does config live — file, env var, flag service, CMS?
Who can change a value, and is each change audited?
What happens if a value is missing or invalid?
Is there a safe default for every config key?
